coredata.dc:title: Novel prussian blue@Carbon-dots hybrid thin film: The impact of carbon-dots on material structure and electrochromic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xue
Chu, Jia
Cheng, Yaping
Yang, Fan
Xiong, Shanxin
coredata.dc:description: Herewith, a series of Prussian blue@Carbon-dot (P@C-dot) hybrids were directly grown on FTO substrate by simple, one-step hydrothermal method in different dosage of aqueous dispersion of carbon dots. Being a new class of carbon allotropes, Carbon dot (C-dot) hybridized with Prussian blue (PB) will enhance the electroactivity of the hybrid film. It was found that the incorporation of C-dots influences the appearance color of the sample, the microstrucutre and electrochromic performance of the hybrids. Raman and XPS analysis revealed structural changes of the hybrid with different dosage of C-dots. The P@C-dot hybrid film exhibits a high-contrast electrochromism, fast response time and highly reversible nature compared to the sample without C-dots. This improved performance of P@C-dot hybrids can be attributed to the role of reinforced C-dots in facilitating electron transport and ionic motion in the hybrid film. Thus, the P@C-dot hybrid film provides a versatile and promising application towards the fabrication of smart windows.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Prussian blue
Carbon dot
Electrochromic
Hydrothermal

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesize procedure of P@C-dot hybrid.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectra of P@C-dot hybrid films.

Typical SEM images of (a) P@C-dot-0, (b) P@C-dot-2, (c) P@C-dot-5 and (d) P@C-dot-10 (Inset shows the photographs of as-synthesized hybrid films); EDAX spectrum of the (e) P@C-dot-0, (f) P@C-dot-2, (g) P@C-dot-5 and (h) P@C-dot-10.

(a) XPS survey spectra of P@C-dot-0 and P@C-dot-10 hybrid, Fe 2p XPS spectra of (b) P@C-dot-0 and (c) P@C-dot-10 hybrid, N 1s XPS spectra of (d) P@C-dot-0 and (e) P@C-dot-10 hybrid, C 1s spectra of (f) P@C-dot-0and (g) P@C-dot-10 hybrid.
Cyclic voltammogram curves of (a) P@C-dot-0, (b) P@C-dot-2, (c) P@C-dot-5 and (d) P@C-dot-10 at different scan rates of 10, 20, 50 and 100 mV s−1.

Optical absorption spectra of colored and bleached state of sample (a) P@C-dot-0, (b) P@C-dot-2, (c) P@C-dot-5 and (d) P@C-dot-10; (e) In situ optical responses of P@C-dot hybrid thin films on FTO glass for 30 s per step measured at 695 nm; (f) EIS spectra of P@C-dot hybrid thin films.
Cyclic stability of (a) P@C-dot-0, (b) P@C-dot-2, (c) P@C-dot-5 and (d) P@C-dot-10 tested at +2.2 V and −2.2 V for 2000s.

head.ce:title: Novel prussian blue@Carbon-dots hybrid thin film: The impact of carbon-dots on material structure and electrochromic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi’an University of Science and Technology, Xi’an, 710054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Xi’an University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Coal Researches Exploration and Comprehensive Utilization, Ministry of Land and Resources, Xi’an, 710054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Coal Researches Exploration and Comprehensive Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key laboratory of coal Researches Exploration and comprehensive utilization, Ministry of land and Resources, Xi’an, 710054, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi’an University of Science and Technology, Xi’an, 710054, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Xi’an University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi’an University of Science and Technology, Xi’an, 710054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Xi’an University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herewith, a series of Prussian blue@Carbon-dot (P@C-dot) hybrids were directly grown on FTO substrate by simple, one-step hydrothermal method in different dosage of aqueous dispersion of carbon dots. Being a new class of carbon allotropes, Carbon dot (C-dot) hybridized with Prussian blue (PB) will enhance the electroactivity of the hybrid film. It was found that the incorporation of C-dots influences the appearance color of the sample, the microstrucutre and electrochromic performance of the hybrids. Raman and XPS analysis revealed structural changes of the hybrid with different dosage of C-dots. The P@C-dot hybrid film exhibits a high-contrast electrochromism, fast response time and highly reversible nature compared to the sample without C-dots. This improved performance of P@C-dot hybrids can be attributed to the role of reinforced C-dots in facilitating electron transport and ionic motion in the hybrid film. Thus, the P@C-dot hybrid film provides a versatile and promising application towards the fabrication of smart windows.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochromic materials have attracted much attention due to their potential applications in smart windows, anti-glare mirrors, data storage devices, displays, and sunglasses [1,2]. Electrochromic layer is one of the main components of electrochromic devices, including inorganic metal oxides and organic conductive polymers [3]. Among them, Prussian blue (PB) and its analogues are an important category of electrochromic materials, showing the color change upon electrochemical modulation, which leading to their potential uses in smart window and video display [4–6]. Electrochemical oxidation or reduction of the iron centers in PB compound requires cations to intercalate or deintercalate the structure to maintain charge neutrality in the electrode. PB, known as a classical mix-valence compound, can be represented by the chemical composition of KFeIIIFeII(CN)6 in the “soluble” form. Partial oxidation of this material leads to formation of Prussian Green (PG), can be expressed by the chemical composition of KFe3 III{[FeIII(CN)6]2[FeII(CN)6]} [7–10]. The fully oxidized and reduced form are known as Prussian Yellow (PY) and Prussian White (PW), with the chemical composition of FeIIIFeIII(CN)6 and K2FeIIFeII(CN)6, respectively. These mixed-valence complexes owing to a perovskite structure with the face centered cubic (FCC) lattice, and have a large channel for the diffusion of K+ ions without change in the crystal structure [7,11].

Despite their benefit of low cost and ease of synthesis, the limitation in single color and lower stability associated with the poor conductivity is still prevent the practical use of this material in EC [12–14]. In order to improve the electrical conductivity of PB compounds, employing carbonaceous materials to PB were demonstrated to be a reliable strategy to overcome many of these drawbacks [15–17]. Graphene and graphene oxide nanosheets for example offer both high surface area and conductivity [18–21]. In a study conducted by Shan et al. [18] showed that assembled [PB/PEI–graphene]n multilayer film exhibited excellent electrochromic property and preferable electrocatalytical properties. In another study, Liu et al. [20] prepared graphene oxide sheets (GOs)-prussian blue nanocomposites via a spontaneous redox reaction. The GOs have a synergistic effect with PB, which improves its electrochemical property such as good conductivity and excellent stability. Carbon nanotubes, on the other hand, offer both high surface area and conductivity [22–24]. Carbon nanotubes/Prussian blue (CNTs/PB) films reported by Nossol et al. [22] showed excellent electrochromic performance and improved electrochemical stability. These reports claimed the incorporation of graphene and CNTs improves the reversibility of electron transport and ionic motion by doping. However, the application of graphene and CNTs to PB has been limited by high contact resistance and difficult synthesis routes which constrain the large scale production [25–27]. In this respect, find a new kind of carbon nanomaterial that can be easily synthesized with tailorable surface, uniform size and unique electronic properties may improve the performance of electrochromic materials. In recent years, C-dots with zero-dimensional spherical particles have emerged as promising carbon allotropes due to its non-toxicity, water solubility, high photostability, superior physicochemical properties and excellent electrochemical properties [28–33]. The prominent feature of C-dots has been proved in many electronic applications such as light emitting diodes, solar cells, sensors, supercapacitors and electrocatalysts [34–38].

So far, no work has been done on the effect of C-dots to the electrochromic properties of PB analogues. Here, we aim to understand the influence of C-dots on the electrochromic performance of PB with different dosage of C-dots at the same synthesis conditions. In doing so, we chose hydrothermal synthesis that can produce high quality crystalline material. Thus, we fabricate the P@C-dot hybrid film directly on the FTO substrate by one-step hydrothermal procedure, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The C-dots were prepared using Citric acid and Ethylenediamine as the raw materials. The P@C-dot hybrid thin films were directly grown on FTO glass by hydrothermal method. Further, we use various characterization techniques including scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diﬀraction (XRD), Raman spectra, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to relate the structural variation and the changes of the electrochromic performance. The incorporation of C-dots to PB shows enhanced optical contrast, fast response time and better cycling stability for P@C-dot hybrid, which showed a promising prospect for application in smart windows.

FTO glasses were obtained from Yingkou opit new energy technology Co., Ltd, China. Citric acid monohydrate (CA), Potassium ferricyanide and Glucose were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents Factory and Ethylenediamine(EDA) was purchased from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagents Factory. Acetone, ethanol, and concentrated hydrochloric acid were obtained from Local reagent supplier. All reagents were analytical grade without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout this study.

The C-dots were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal approach as reported with modification [39]. Briefly, 3.153 g of CA and 1 ml of EDA were dissolved in 30 ml of deionized water and continuously stirred for 20 min. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. After reacting at 180 °C for 24 h, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. Then the C-dots were separated by dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) with deionized water for 2 days. C-dots solution was then collected and used thereof.

FTO glass were ultrasonically cleaned in acetone, ethanol and distilled water separately, and then dried in air before use. In a typical synthesis, 0.66 g of potassium ferricyanide and 0.25 g of glucose were mixed with different dosage of C-dots (0, 2, 5, and 10 ml of C-dots aqueous solution, respectively) in different volume of deionized water. 0.4 ml of concentrated hydrochloric acid was added dropwise to the solution under magnetic stirring. After stirring for another 20 min, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave containing a piece of FTO glass with the conducting surface facing down. Then the autoclave was sealed and heated at 120 °C for 1 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the FTO glass coated with thin films were taken out and rinsed with deionized water several times and dried in air. Finally, the as-synthesized samples with 0, 2, 5, and 10 ml of C-dots are designated as P@C-dot-0, P@C-dot-2, P@C-dot-5 and P@C-dot-10, respectively.

The phase compositions of the P@C-dot hybrid film were characterized by XRD technique (Rigaku MiniFlex 600, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation in the 2θ range of 3–80°. The morphology of the samples was monitored by SEM (Hitachi S-4800, Japan). Raman spectra were recorded by a Raman spectrometer (Invia Renishaw, U.K) using a 532 nm wavelength excitation light. The chemical composition and valence states of constituent elements were analyzed by XPS (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha instrument, USA). All electrochemical and electrochromic properties of the film were investigated by using an Autolab PGSTAT302N (Metrohm Autolab, The Netherlands) electrochemical workstation with an UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV-2550, Japan) over the wavelength range from 400 to 800 nm. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was carried out in a standard three-electrode system, where the as-synthesized P@C-dot hybrid thin film of 1 × 4 cm2 effective area was used as the working electrode, the Pt sheet as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode in a cuvette containing 1 M KCl electrolyte. The scan range is from −0.5 to 1.6 V with a scanning rate of 0.1 V s−1. Electrochemical impedance (EIS) was recorded in the frequency range of 10−2 -105 Hz.

The XRD patterns of the as-synthesized P@C-dot hybrid thin films and bare FTO glass are shown in Fig. 2 a. All the diffraction peaks of P@C-dot-0, P@C-dot-2, P@C-dot-5 and P@C-dot-10 hybrid films are quite similar, which indicates that the samples are the same kind of crystallinity. The peaks for all as-prepared P@C-dot hybrid films present sharp peaks, suggesting they are well crystallized. After compositing with C-dots, the diffraction patterns of P@C-dot-2, P@C-dot-5 and P@C-dot-10 show apparent peaks at 17.4° (200) and 24.6° (220) (JCPDS card No.73–0687), which can be indexed to the FCC phase of PB. It is worth noting that no obvious diffraction peaks for C-dots can be observed and all peaks of the as-synthesized P@C-dot hybrid thin films display almost the same characteristic peaks as hydrothermally grown PB film in our previous work except for minor difference in the diffraction strength [40]. This may due to the small amount of C-dots in the hybrid. Nevertheless, the presence of C-dots can be verified by Raman and XPS, which are more sensitive to identify the presence of C-dots in the hybrid.

Fig. 2b displays the Raman spectra of P@C-dot hybrid thin films. The absorption peaks located at 271 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretched vibration of Fe–N bond. The peak at 540 cm−1 of P@C-dot-0 corresponds to the Fe–C stretching bending mode. The strong peak at 2155 cm−1 is related to the stretching mode of C–N group. With the increase of C-dots, the intensity of all the peaks gradually increases, this indicates that C-dots enhance the Raman peak intensities of the hybrid, which may attributed to the surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) effect [41]. As expected, the spectrum of P@C-dot-10 displays two prominent bands at 1360 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, which typically observed in carbon materials and correspond to the well documented D and G modes of C-dots, respectively [8]. Therefore, as indicated by the above Raman spectra results, the incorporation of C-dots in P@C-dot hybrid films has been successfully proved.

Fig. 3 shows the representative SEM images of P@C-dot-0 and other samples with various C-dots (P@C-dot-2, P@C-dot-5 and P@C-dot-10). A typical image of P@C-dot-0 is seen in Fig. 3a, which shows a regular cubic structure with the sizes in the range of 50–300 nm all over the FTO surface. The hybrids of P@C-dot-2 and P@C-dot-5 show similar morphologies to that of PB nanocubes, while the smooth surface observed on P@C-dot-5 hybrid structures are noteworthy, as shown in Fig. 3b and c. In the case of the highest content of C-dots, the nanocubes of P@C-dot-10 hybrid with collapse angles are stacked tightly and as a result the particle size of the nanocubes decreased, as shown in Fig. 3d. We attribute the differences in the morphologies of the hybrid as being due to the C-dots interconnection with the PB nanocubes during the hydrothermal assembly process. These results depict that by controlling the content of C-dots, we are able to achieve different morphological heterostructural hybrid materials for electrochromic application. Photographs of as-synthesized hybrid film shown in inset of Fig. 3a–d clearly reveal that an uniform and dense layer of P@C-dot hybrid have been coated on FTO substrate with obvious appearance color changes of light yellow-green for P@C-dot-0, green for P@C-dot-2, blue-green for P@C-dot-5 and blue for P@C-dot-10. The color changes of P@C-dot hybrid films indicate the C-dots were successfully introduced into the hybrids. EDAX measurement indicated the presence of the following element: Fe, C, N and O for the hybrid film. The atomic% and weight% of the above mentioned elements have been shown in Fig. 3 e-h. When increasing the concentration of C-dots, the weight% of C element for P@C-dot hybrids increases from 17.04% to 22.44%, which further confirms the incorporation of C-dots into the hybrid film.

To better clarify the surface chemical composition of the hybrids and the valence states of various species, XPS analysis of P@C-dot-0 and P@C-dot-10 hybrids were carried out. Fig. 4 a is the typical survey spectra of P@C-dot-0 and P@C-dot-10, showing the presence of Fe 2p, N 1s and C 1s from the samples and their surface, and O 1s from the absorbed gaseous molecules. Fig. 4b shows the high resolution Fe 2p spectra of the P@C-dot-0 hybrid, the binding energies of Fe 2p 1/2 and Fe 2p 3/2 were observed at 720–725 region and 711.7 eV, which are attributed to Fe3+ ions in the hybrid [42]. In addition, peaks at 707.9 and 709.3 eV can be assigned to Fe 2p 3/2(Fe2+) of [Fe(CN)6]4-, indicating the formation of a mixed-valence hybrid [43]. Whereas in case of P@C-dot-10 hybrid, the binding energies of Fe 2p 3/2 and Fe 2p 1/2 observed at 719.6 and 710.6, respectively, originated from the presence of Fe3+ ions. Furthermore, the peak centered at 706.6 eV can be assigned to Fe2+ ions, as shown in Fig. 4c. Fig. 4d shows the high resolution N 1s spectra of the P@C-dot-0, the binding energies at 397.2 eV related to Fe–CN and another peak at 398.9 eV is due to pyrrolic N [42]. However, the N1s spectra of P@C-dot-10 showed the peak with a binding energy of 398.3 eV, which originated from the nitrogen functional groups of the C-dots and indicates the efficient incorporation of C-dot into PB, as shown in Fig. 4e. The C 1s XPS spectra of the hybrids were examined to gain a better understanding of the incorporation of C-dots in the P@C-dot hybrid films. The deconvolution of C 1s peaks of P@C-dot-0 (Fig. 4f) at 284.1eV originates from C–C bond while the peak at 285.7 eV can be associated with C–N bond [44,45]. For P@C-dot-10, it is noteworthy that the peak being at 283.1, 284.8 and 286.4 eV were observed (Fig. 4g), which were attributed to the Fe–C, C–C and C–N bond, respectively. The presence of C–N groups confirms the strong electronic interaction between C-dots and PB in the hybrid [46].

Proposed formation mechanism of Prussian blue analogues are as follows: firstly, owing to the decomposition of [Fe(CN)6]3- ions (potassium ferricyanide) in the presence of H+ ions (hydrochloric acid), Fe3+ ions are released [47]; then, a certain amount of [Fe(CN)6]3- ions are reduced to [Fe(CN)6]4- ions by glucose [48]. subsequently, a part of the reduced [Fe(CN)6]4- ions were directly combined with Fe3+ ion to form a certain amount of FeIII[FeII(CN)6]- ions; Finally, through the precipitation reaction of Fe3+, [Fe(CN)6]3- and FeIII[FeII(CN)], Prussian blue are grown [49–52]. In our study, the C-dots expected to be bonded with the ions and participate in each reaction process. The results of XRD, Raman, SEM and XPS strongly confirmed the involvement of C-dots to the formation of heterostructural P@C-dot hybrid thin films.

To understand the electrochemical behavior of the P@C-dot hybrid films, the CV was estimated with scan rates of 10, 20, 50, and 100 mVs−1 under potentials between 0.4 and 1.5 V, as shown in Fig. 5 . The P@C-dot-0 present two pairs of redox peaks (O1/R1 at 0.314/0.08 V and O2/R2 at 0.97/0.758 V) which can be attributed to the reduction/oxidation of Fe3+/Fe2+ ions during the insertion and extraction of K+ ions (Fig. 5a). The possible mechanism for charge compensation in PG/PB redox activity is the entrapment of anions in course of oxidative reaction (Eq.(1) and (2)) [53].

During the CV measurements, the P@C-dot-0, P@C-dot-2 and P@C-dot-5 hybrid films exhibit reversible color changes from green (PG) to transparent (PW) then to blue (PB) (Fig. 5a–c). With the increasing of the scan rates, the positions of oxidation and reduction peaks shift toward a more oxidation and reduction peaks direction. For P@C-dot-5 and P@C-dot-10, the oxidation peak between 0.8 and 1.2 V gradually disappeared at higher scan rates, such a change may be due to the fast electron transfer among the heterostructure and the fast redox reactions occur in the P@C-dot hybrid films. In addition, P@C-dot hybrid electrode exhibits broader redox peak than that of P@C-dot-0, indicating the effective incorporation of C-dots into PB could change the oxidation degree and ratio of Fe3+/Fe2+ ions. Obviously, the redox areas inside the CV curves of P@C-dot-2 and P@C-dot-5 hybrid electrode are larger compared to that of P@C-dot-0 and P@C-dot-10, indicating the high surface area and the edge effect of the C-dots along with the intrinsic defects can provide an effective charge transfer and rapid ion diffusion.

The absorption spectra in the wavelength range of 400–800 nm in colored and bleached states of the samples are shown in Fig. 6 . As depicted in Fig. 6a–d, with the increase concentration of C-dots, the optical contrast of the P@C-dot-0, P@C-dot-2, P@C-dot-5 and P@C-dot-10 increases in turn, which are 2.0, 2.1, 2.8 and 3.3, respectively. This phenomenon is consistent with the deepen of color from light yellow-green to blue. The coloration/bleaching time (tc/tb) of the hybrid thin films can be estimated according to the time required for achieving 90% of their total absorbance changes at 695 nm. The tc/tb of the P@C-dot-0, P@C-dot-2, P@C-dot-5 and P@C-dot-10 films are 11.0/7.1, 11.8/8.8, 12.6/12.2 and 14.8/14.0 s, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6e. Compared to other electrochromic properties, an interesting phenomenon during the optical switching process of the P@C-dot-5 was the appearance of a small spike when the potential is converted from anode to cathode. When applying +2.2 V, the P@C-dot-5 film is blue, after the voltage is converted to −2.2 V, P@C-dot-5 film will be colored slightly before bleaching because a large amount of intercalated ions are accumulated at short time [54]. These results demonstrate that P@C-dot heterostructures possess great potential toward developing high-performance multi-color devices.

Advanced characterization techniques such as EIS can reveal detailed information on the electrical transport properties. In order to demonstrate the electrochemical properties of the P@C-dot heterostructures, EIS was carried out in the frequency range of 10−2-105 Hz, as shown in Fig. 6f. It can be seen that all Nyquist plots of the hybrid are consist of semicircles in the high frequency region and the slopes of the straight line in the low frequency region represent charge transfer resistance (Rct) and diffusion resistance. The Rct critically influences the switching speed of electrochromic materials. The lower Rct of the hybrid enables fast redox reactions and easier electron transfer, and thus improved its electrochromic performance. It is clearly presented that the phase angles of the P@C-dot-2 and P@C-dot-5 approach nearly to 90°, which exhibit lower solution resistance among other hybrid films. This is mainly due to the lower charge transfer resistance and easier diffusion of the ions. This result confirmed that the incorporation of C-dots could improve the electrical conductivity and also form a complex synergistic effect between strongly interacting of PB and C-dots.

The stability of the P@C-dot hybrid films were inquired with an alternate application of voltage +2.2 V and −2.2 V at a monochromatic wavelength of 695 nm, as shown in Fig. 7 . A switching time of 20 s was chosen as being in excess of the time required for each film to reach 100% of the full change in absorbance after the switching of the potential. Fig. 7 shows voltage induced absorbance change of the as-synthesized films for scanning period of 2000 s. The P@C-dot-2, P@C-dot-5 and P@C-dot-10 hybrid films exhibit higher durability over the entire scanning period, in contrast to the P@C-dot-0 film. Remarkably, the contrast of P@C-dot-2 was very stable without significant change over 2000 s upon repetitive color/bleach switching. The best performance for P@C-dot-2 indicates the achievement of the maximum synergistic effect between the C-dot and PB instead of the amount of a single component. The remarkable performance was presumably due to the synergistic effect between the C-dots and PB which offers more convenient for electron transport and ion motion.

P@C-dots thin films have been successfully synthesized for electrochromic performance by hydrothermal method. The C-dots cause differences in the visible appearance color of the hybrid thin film and also determine the microstructure of the hybrid. With the synergistic effect arising from the C-dots and PB as well as the efficient charge transfer ability, the hybrid show superior electrochromic performance. The P@C-dot-2 thin film exhibits best electrochromic behavior with high optical modulation, fast coloration/bleaching response and better stability. Thus, the unique approach for developing hybrids from C-dots and PB opens a new avenue for the rational design of C-dots based electrochromic materials for electrochromic uses in energy saving smart window. Apart from the electrochromic electrode in this study, the results show that this exploration would help to extend the application of P@C-dot hybrid in the field of biodetector, electrochemical sensor, electrocatalyst, supercapacitor, battery and so on.

Xue Li: Writing - original draft, Data curation, and Formal analysis. Jia Chu: Funding acquisition, Writing - original draft, and Investigation. Yaping Cheng: Methodology, and Formal analysis. Fan Yang: Investigation, and Supervision. Shanxin Xiong: Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 51503169) and the project of Excellent Youth Scientific Research Foundation of Xi’an University of Science and Technology (No. 2019YQ2-10).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile solution synthesis of tungsten trioxide doped with nanocrystalline molybdenum trioxide for electrochromic devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Hasani, Q.V. Le, T.P. Nguyen, K.S. Choi, W. Sohn, J.K. Kim, H.W. Jang, S.Y. Kim, Facile Solution Synthesis of Tungsten Trioxide Doped with Nanocrystalline Molybdenum Trioxide for Electrochromic Devices, Sci Rep, 7 (2017) 13258.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-supported one-dimensional materials for enhanced electrochromism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Tong, S. Liu, X. Li, J. Zhao, Y. Li, Self-supported one-dimensional materials for enhanced electrochromism, Nanoscale Horizons, 3 (2018) 261-292.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Organic/inorganic electrochromic nanocomposites with various interfacial interactions: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.X. Xiong, S.Y. Yin, Y.Y. Wang, Z.Z. Kong, J.P. Lan, R.L. Zhang, M. Gong, B.H. Wu, J. Chu, X.Q. Wang, Organic/inorganic electrochromic nanocomposites with various interfacial interactions: A review, Materials Science and Engineering: B, 221 (2017) 41-53.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: All solid-state electrochromic window of Prussian Blue and electrodeposited WO3 film with PMMA gel electrolyte

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Su, Q. Hong, Z. Lu, All solid-state electrochromic window of Prussian Blue and electrodeposited WO3 film with PMMA gel electrolyte, Materials Chemistry & Physics, 56 (1998) 266-270.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Absorption spectra of an electrochromic window based on molybdovanadophosphoric acid, Prussian Blue and a solid polymer electrolyte
